---
title: "Interview Problems — Bayesian Reasoning"
subtitle: "The conceptual questions an interviewer actually asks, and one whiteboard problem worked out loud."
---

::: {.field-notes-band}
<div class="fn-eyebrow">Lesson 01 · Interview preparation</div>
<div class="fn-title">At the Whiteboard: Bayesian Reasoning</div>
<div class="fn-meta">
  <span class="fn-tag">Conceptual questions</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">Model answers</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">One live whiteboard problem</span>
</div>
:::

The [exercise set](bayes-in-action.qmd#exercise-set) drills the *computation*.
This page drills the *conversation* — the questions a technical interviewer asks
to find out whether you understand what you're computing. Read each question,
say your answer out loud before you expand the model answer, and pay attention
to the **reasoning**, not just the punchline. Interviewers are listening for how
you think.

::: {.lens}
Across these questions, the interviewer is probing one thing: *do you treat a
number as an answer, or as a claim that has assumptions and can be wrong?* The
strongest candidates state their assumptions unprompted, name the base rate,
and volunteer a sensitivity check. That habit is worth more than any single
correct number.
:::

## Conceptual questions

::: {.callout-important collapse="true"}
## Q1 — Define a prior, a likelihood, and a posterior in one breath each.
**Model answer.** The **prior** $P(H)$ is what you believe about a hypothesis
before seeing the new evidence — often the base rate. The **likelihood**
$P(E\mid H)$ is how well the evidence is predicted by that hypothesis (note the
direction of the bar: probability *of the evidence*, *given* the hypothesis).
The **posterior** $P(H\mid E)$ is your updated belief after folding the evidence
into the prior. Bayes' theorem is just the rule that combines them:
$P(H\mid E)\propto P(E\mid H)\,P(H)$.

*What they're checking:* that you don't confuse the likelihood
$P(E\mid H)$ with the posterior $P(H\mid E)$ — the single most common error in
the whole subject.
:::

::: {.callout-important collapse="true"}
## Q2 — A diagnostic is "99% accurate." A patient tests positive. Is their probability of disease 99%?
**Model answer.** No — and the word "accurate" is doing suspicious work. You
need three numbers, not one: the **sensitivity**, the **specificity**, and,
above all, the **prevalence** (base rate). For a rare disease, false positives
come from a huge healthy population and easily outnumber true positives, so the
posterior can be far below 99% — often under 20%. The correct response is to ask
for the prevalence and then count out of a concrete population (say 10,000).

*What they're checking:* the base-rate reflex. Immediately asking "how common is
the disease?" is the answer they want to hear.
:::

::: {.callout-important collapse="true"}
## Q3 — Your classifier has 95% sensitivity and 95% specificity. You deploy it to flag a condition present in 1 of every 500 samples. What precision do you expect?
**Model answer.** Precision *is* $P(\text{true}\mid\text{flagged})$ — a Bayesian
posterior. Per 100,000 samples: 200 positives → 190 true positives; 99,800
negatives → 4,990 false positives. Precision $= 190/(190+4990)\approx 3.7\%$.
Roughly 26 false alarms per real hit. Mitigations: raise the decision threshold
(trade recall for precision), **enrich** the screened population to lift the
prior, add a confirmatory second stage, or report PR-AUC rather than ROC-AUC
because ROC hides this on imbalanced data.

*What they're checking:* that you connect "base rate" to the very concrete
production metric of precision, and that you have real mitigations — not just
a lament.
:::

::: {.callout-important collapse="true"}
## Q4 — What's the difference between a frequentist and a Bayesian answer to "does this drug work?"
**Model answer.** The frequentist reports $P(\text{data at least this extreme}
\mid \text{no effect})$ — a *p*-value — and controls long-run error rates; it
never assigns a probability to the hypothesis itself. The Bayesian reports
$P(\text{effect}\mid\text{data})$ directly, but must supply a prior to do so.
With abundant data they usually converge; they diverge most when data is scarce
or the prior is influential — and that's exactly when stating the prior
explicitly is a virtue, not a weakness [@gelman2013].

*What they're checking:* that you can hold both frameworks without tribalism,
and that you know a *p*-value is not $P(H_0\mid\text{data})$.
:::

::: {.callout-important collapse="true"}
## Q5 — How does Bayesian reasoning explain why "most published research findings are false"?
**Model answer.** Treat each tested hypothesis as a diagnostic test. The
**prior** is the fraction of tested hypotheses that are genuinely true (in an
exploratory field, low). A "significant" result is a **positive test** with its
own false-positive rate ($\alpha$) and true-positive rate (power). If few
hypotheses are true and power is mediocre, the false positives dominate the
significant results — so the posterior probability that a significant finding is
real can fall below 50% [@ioannidis2005]. Bias and multiple testing push it
lower still. This is precisely the rare-disease figure, relabeled, and it
motivates false-discovery-rate control [@benjamini1995].

*What they're checking:* that you can transfer the base-rate argument from
diagnostics to inference — the bridge to the multiple-testing lesson.
:::

::: {.callout-important collapse="true"}
## Q6 — When would you *not* trust a Bayesian analysis?
**Model answer.** When the conclusion is driven by the prior rather than the
data, and the prior is not defensible. The honest tool is a **sensitivity
analysis**: vary the prior across a reasonable range and report how much the
posterior moves. If the conclusion flips under plausible priors, say the
evidence is weak. Also be wary of priors smuggled in as "objective" defaults,
and of likelihoods that assume an independence that doesn't hold (the naive-Bayes
overconfidence problem).

*What they're checking:* intellectual honesty. Volunteering the failure mode of
your own method is a strong signal.
:::

## The whiteboard problem

Here is a problem posed the way it would be in a live interview — vague on
purpose, so that *asking the right clarifying question is part of the answer*.
Try it before expanding the walkthrough.

::: {.whiteboard}
### Prompt

> "We built a screen for a rare regulatory variant. It calls a variant present
> or absent. On our validation set it's 98% sensitive and 96% specific. A
> collaborator runs it genome-wide and is thrilled — thousands of hits. Should
> they be? Walk me through how you'd think about it."

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Model train of thought (expand only after trying)
**1. Ask for the base rate.** "Rare" is the whole game. If the variant occurs at,
say, 1 in 10,000 sites, that's the prior. Nothing else matters until I have it.

**2. Count out of a concrete population.** Per 1,000,000 sites at a 1/10,000 base
rate: 100 true sites → 98 true positives. 999,900 non-sites → 4% false-positive
rate → ~39,996 false positives. So ~40,094 total hits, of which 98 are real.

**3. State the posterior / precision.** $P(\text{real}\mid\text{hit}) \approx
98/40{,}094 \approx 0.24\%$. The "thousands of hits" are almost entirely false
positives. The collaborator's excitement is measuring the false-positive rate,
not a discovery.

**4. Anticipate the objection.** "But it's 96% specific!" — right, and at this
base rate a 4% false-positive rate applied to a million sites still buries 98
true hits. Specificity has to be extraordinary (or the search space small) for
rare-event precision to be usable.

**5. Offer mitigations, ranked.** (a) Reduce the search space with prior
biology — restrict to plausible regions, which raises the effective prior.
(b) Add an orthogonal confirmatory assay: each independent positive multiplies
the odds by its likelihood ratio, and two cheap tests can move 0.24% to
something trustworthy. (c) Raise the calling threshold and quantify the
recall you sacrifice. (d) Report expected precision up front so no one mistakes
40,000 hits for 40,000 findings.

**6. Close with the sensitivity check.** "If the true base rate is anywhere from
1 in 1,000 to 1 in 100,000, precision ranges from roughly 2.4% down to 0.02% —
so my conclusion is robust: without a confirmatory step, these hits can't be
trusted." Naming that range unprompted is the mark of a strong answer.
:::
:::

::: {.lens}
Notice what carried that answer: not the arithmetic (which is a 2×2 table) but
the *moves* — ask for the base rate, count concretely, translate to precision,
rank mitigations, end with sensitivity. Those five moves generalize to almost
every applied-statistics interview question. Bank them.
:::

::: {.lesson-nav}
<span>[← Bayes in Action](bayes-in-action.qmd)</span>
<span>[Back to the lesson plan](index.qmd)</span>
:::

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
